monday.label: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
monday.description: Local arts scene, festivals, creative economy, media, and cultural tech
monday.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
monday.scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' — how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Most genuine arts/culture/media journalism should land well above 0, even without a Cariboo tie-in:
- 80-100: Cariboo/Williams Lake-specific arts and culture news — community arts council grants or murals, local festivals, Indigenous film/storytelling, community theatre, museum/heritage programs, a Cariboo author or artist receiving an award, community radio or local publishing.
- 55-79: Rural BC or Canadian arts/culture journalism without a specific Cariboo tie — CBC Arts features on Indigenous storytelling, regional festivals, community media projects, heritage preservation, creative-economy stories in small towns, digital storytelling tools and platforms.
- 30-54: General arts, culture, media, or entertainment journalism with real creative/storytelling substance but no rural or community angle (national awards shows, mainstream film/TV/music industry coverage, publishing industry news).
- 15-29: Tangentially creative content (a tech feature that touches on media/publishing/streaming) or local Cariboo civic news with no arts/culture angle.
- 0-14: No arts, culture, media, or storytelling dimension at all.

Strong fits include: community arts journalism (arts councils, local festivals, heritage programs), oral history and cultural preservation projects, Indigenous film and storytelling, community theatre and local media, rural arts funding and the creative economy in small towns, and digital storytelling platforms and tools. Also strong fits: community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. A story about a Cariboo community mural, a CBC Arts feature on BC Indigenous storytelling, a rural arts council receiving grants, or a Cariboo author/artist shortlisted for an award is a strong fit even without an explicit technology angle — cultural journalism matters to this audience. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): medical and health research with no cultural/storytelling angle; working lands and resource economy stories with no cultural framing; wildlife and nature stories without creative or community media context; data centre infrastructure and AI investment news; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime coverage; financial market and stock movement stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

tuesday.label: Working Lands & Industry
tuesday.description: Cariboo forestry, ranching, mining, agriculture — tech in the resource economy
tuesday.theme_description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both.
tuesday.scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' — the resource economy that defines the Cariboo and rural BC.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Trade-press journalism about forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture should score well even when it's equipment-, market-, or operations-focused rather than policy-focused — a story about a piece of farm machinery or a mining operation update is a real fit, not a 0:
- 80-100: BC/Cariboo-specific forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture stories (mill closures, beetle kill, BCTS, Cariboo ranchers and water rights, local mine operations, Cariboo Cattlemen events).
- 55-79: Canadian resource-economy trade journalism without a BC tie — Western Producer or Northern Miner-style coverage of farm equipment and machinery, harvest technology, livestock markets, mining operations and exploration, AgTech, forestry practices, sawmills, or labour/commodity stories in the resource sector.
- 30-54: Broader agriculture, forestry, ranching, or mining news (e.g. international resource-industry stories, general farm economics, climate impacts on working lands) with clear working-lands relevance but no Canadian/BC tie.
- 15-29: Tangential resource-sector mentions (e.g. a general business, policy, or science story that touches briefly on forestry/agriculture/mining) or local Cariboo civic news unrelated to working lands.
- 0-14: No connection to forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture.

Strong fits include: BC and Canadian forestry journalism (mill closures, lumber markets, beetle kill, harvest operations, silviculture, BCTS, old-growth policy), ranching and livestock stories (water rights, grazing management, cattle markets, drought impact on ranches), mining and resource extraction (safety, new operations, Indigenous consultation, supply chains, exploration updates), agriculture in rural Canada (crop insurance, grain prices, farm economics, food sovereignty, farm equipment and machinery), and the technology helping these industries adapt — precision forestry, AgTech, drone surveys, IoT in agriculture, mine automation, sawmill modernization, climate adaptation on working lands. Also strong fits: labor and workforce stories in resource industries, environmental stewardship within working landscapes (not pure conservation, but how ranchers/loggers manage for sustainability), and science with direct implications for forestry, agriculture, or extractive industries. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): international geopolitical and war news with no resource-economy connection; consumer tech and gadget reviews; pure wildlife entertainment without agricultural or working-lands relevance; crime and judicial news unrelated to resource industries; financial market and stock ticker stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

wednesday.label: Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech
wednesday.description: Consumer hardware, repair culture, DIY projects, maker tech, and practical everyday tools
wednesday.theme_description: Today we look at the tools, gadgets, and DIY projects that make everyday life more capable — from repairable electronics and maker hardware to practical consumer tech and creative builds. We're interested in the intersection of tinkering culture and practical utility: things you can fix, build, or repurpose, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where self-sufficiency matters.
wednesday.scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Gear, Gadgets & Practical Tech' — consumer hardware, DIY maker culture, repairability, and practical tools. Strong fits include: gadget and device reviews, right-to-repair news and teardowns, maker and DIY project guides, 3D printing hardware news, practical consumer electronics (smartphones, laptops, headphones, wearables), off-grid and outdoor tech gear, workshop tools and home improvement tech, hardware hacks and creative builds, battery and power tech, and general how-to-build/fix/improve-things content. Prefer articles that are hands-on, practical, or focused on the maker and repair community. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): geopolitical conflict, war, terrorism, and international news with no hardware or maker angle; natural disaster coverage without a practical engineering or preparedness tech angle; wildlife and conservation stories; working lands policy; Indigenous governance; scientific discoveries without a hands-on hardware or DIY element; financial market and investment news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

thursday.label: Indigenous Lands & Innovation
thursday.description: Tŝilhqot'in, Secwépemc, Dakelh — language, governance, land stewardship, and tech
thursday.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it.
thursday.scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Indigenous Lands & Innovation' — Indigenous nations using technology, governance, and stewardship on their own terms.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Indigenous-led journalism on governance, rights, and self-determination should score in the moderate-to-good range even without an explicit technology angle — do not collapse everything that lacks a 'tech' hook to near 0:
- 70-100: Cariboo-specific stories bridging Indigenous governance/culture AND technology — Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh language revitalization tech, guardian programs using sensors/drones, GIS land mapping, Indigenous-led connectivity, digital cultural heritage projects.
- 45-69: Pan-Canadian Indigenous governance, sovereignty, land rights, treaty, or reconciliation journalism — APTN, Eagle Feather News, or IndigiNews-style coverage of First Nations governance, MMIWG, land claims, self-determination, or Indigenous-led services — even without a technology angle, per the curator's standing interest in Indigenous governance and sovereignty.
- 25-44: Indigenous cultural, community, or human-interest stories (awards, events, ceremonies) with limited governance or tech substance, or BC land-stewardship/environmental stories with meaningful First Nations involvement.
- 10-24: General BC land, environmental, or forestry stories that mention Indigenous communities only in passing.
- 0-9: No Indigenous angle at all.

Strong fits include: language revitalization technology for Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh, Indigenous guardian programs using remote monitoring and sensors, land management tech and environmental monitoring on Indigenous lands, digital tools for traditional knowledge preservation, community mapping and GIS for land claims, Indigenous-led connectivity projects, technology for cultural heritage documentation, mobile apps for language learning, drone/satellite monitoring for stewardship, reconciliation through digital collaboration, how Cariboo Indigenous nations use innovation for self-determination, and Indigenous governance, sovereignty, treaty, and land-rights journalism from any Canadian nation. Prefer articles that bridge Indigenous governance/culture AND technology, but governance/sovereignty journalism alone is a solid fit. Penalise heavily (score 0–9): international geopolitical and war news with no Indigenous rights angle; general wildfire and BC Wildlife Service updates not led by or directly impacting Indigenous communities or guardians; provincial forestry and environmental policy without explicit First Nations involvement; consumer tech and gadget reviews; science discoveries without Indigenous stewardship or sovereignty context; crime and judicial news unrelated to Indigenous rights. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

friday.label: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
friday.description: Chilcotin wilderness, backcountry recreation, conservation tech, wildfire and climate
friday.theme_description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed.
friday.scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: wildfire detection tech and BC Wildfire monitoring systems, satellite/drone surveillance for Chilcotin wilderness, wildlife tracking and conservation technology, watershed sensors and salmon habitat monitoring, weather stations and climate tracking in backcountry, avalanche forecasting tech, trail mapping and GPS systems, outdoor recreation apps and connectivity, backcountry communication devices (satellite messengers, Meshtastic), flood/drought prediction systems, remote cameras for wildlife research, and how technology helps manage and protect Cariboo wild spaces. Prefer articles that bridge wilderness/conservation AND technology. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): prehistoric and ancient biology (fossils, palaeontology, deep-time creatures) with no contemporary conservation technology angle — these belong to Sunday's Science theme; AI and data centre infrastructure stories about business strategy, investment, or corporate politics rather than direct wilderness, ecosystem, or land-use impact in BC; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime news with no outdoor or conservation relevance; consumer tech reviews and financial market stories; general political and judicial news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

saturday.label: Cariboo Local Affairs
saturday.description: Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola — community stories and digital equity
saturday.theme_description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC.
saturday.scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Cariboo Voices & Local News' — stories FROM and ABOUT the Cariboo-Chilcotin region and its communities. Score 80-100 for articles directly covering: news and events in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, Horsefly, Likely, Anahim Lake, Alexis Creek, or the Cariboo Regional District; local government and civic decisions (City of Williams Lake, City of Quesnel, CRD, District of 100 Mile House); local health services and emergency closures; local economy, businesses, and community programs; Cariboo arts, culture, and community events; AND digital equity, rural internet access (fiber, fixed wireless, Starlink), and connectivity serving these specific communities. Articles from local Cariboo news sources (mycariboonow.com, wltribune.com, quesnelobserver.com, 100milefreepress.net, cfjctoday.com) covering Cariboo community life should score 65-85. Score 50-79 for: BC provincial news directly affecting Cariboo communities, rural Canadian community stories closely paralleling Cariboo life, digital equity and rural internet broadly. Score 20-49 for: general BC news that may affect rural areas, broader rural Canada topics without specific Cariboo tie-in. Score 0-19 for: national/international news without Cariboo relevance, pure tech or entertainment with no community angle. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no Cariboo tie-in; science discoveries without a local community connection; consumer tech reviews and gadget guides; wildlife and conservation stories not involving Cariboo communities or First Nations; BC provincial news without direct Cariboo-region relevance. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

sunday.label: Science, Wonder & the Natural World
sunday.description: Scientific discovery, natural phenomena, ecology, and the curiosity behind how our world works — grounded in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo
sunday.theme_description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works.
sunday.scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Science, Wonder & the Natural World' — scientific discovery, ecology, natural phenomena, and the curiosity-driven study of living systems. Strong fits include: wildlife biology and species research, ecology studies and biodiversity findings, watershed and salmon research relevant to BC, astronomy and space science, new species discoveries, citizen science projects, natural history stories, wildfire science and forest ecology, climate science findings (not just policy), geology, evolution, and deep-time Earth stories, and any scientific research grounded in BC or Cariboo landscapes and ecosystems. Prefer articles that convey genuine curiosity and discovery — the 'how' and 'why' of the natural world. Score 70–90 for: prehistoric life and palaeontology (ancient oceans, Cretaceous ecology, deep-time species), new species discoveries in ocean or remote environments, deep-sea biology, evolutionary biology findings, natural history stories with a strong sense of wonder, and field research grounded in BC ecosystems. Penalise (score 0–30): consumer health and pharmaceutical research without ecology or natural-world context; pure policy and advocacy news; technology business stories without a science or ecology angle; human interest and viral news without scientific substance; geopolitical and crime news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.
